nativeRange.habitat: Coral and rocky reef slopes; typically found at 5–30 m on outer reef faces with strong water movement, though has been documented from 0 to roughly 90 m.

diet.notes: Primarily a grazer of leafy brown macroalgae (Sargassum, Dictyota) in the wild. In captivity, dried marine seaweed (nori) offered daily on a clip is generally advised as a dietary staple, supplemented with herbivore pellets/flakes and Spirulina. Mysis or other meaty foods have been reported to be accepted opportunistically but should not dominate the diet. Frequent algae feeding (3+ times per day for an active adult) tends to support health, reduce aggression, and help prevent Head and Lateral Line Erosion.

avoidWith: other tangs and surgeonfish (especially same genus)
conspecifics in anything but very large systems
very small or timid fish that may be intimidated by its size

summary: A large Indo-Pacific surgeonfish prized for its black face mask, orange lips, and bright orange caudal-peduncle spines. Generally considered reef-safe but reaches roughly 45 cm and is best suited to very large, well-oxygenated systems.
careNotes: Naso lituratus is the Pacific 'orangespine unicornfish'; the visually similar Indian Ocean form is now generally recognized as a separate species, Naso elegans, though trade-name overlap (especially 'Orange-Spine Unicornfish') is common. Adults reach approximately 45 cm and are powerful, near-constant swimmers — guidance for long-term housing tends toward tanks of around 1000 L (or larger) with a long horizontal footprint, high turnover (~10x), strong protein skimming, and saturated oxygenation. Hobby sources (LiveAquaria, Tropical Fish Keeping) list a 180-gallon (~680 L) minimum, while specialist sources (Reef2Reef, TFH Magazine) recommend 240–500+ gallons (~900–1900 L); the conservative-larger figure used here reflects the species' adult size, swimming demands, and a 'specialist-only' suitability for typical hobbyists. The caudal-peduncle keels (scalpels) are permanently erect, brightly colored orange, and can inflict serious cuts to handlers or tear nets — handling caution has been widely emphasized. Juveniles are dark gray with a yellow dorsal stripe and blue accents; adults develop a striking black face mask outlined in yellow, reddish-orange lips, a brownish-gray body, and males grow long lyre-shaped caudal-fin streamers. Has been reported to be susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) in tanks with poor water quality or vitamin-poor diets, and to ich/marine velvet — quarantine and a varied algae-rich diet are generally advised. Jumping has been documented; a tight-fitting lid is considered essential. FishBase wild temperature range (24.5–28.9°C) is somewhat warmer than hobby recommendations (22–26°C); the range used here (23–27°C) averages these. Lifespan reports vary widely from ~8 years in captivity to 30+ in well-kept systems, with anecdotal reports of even longer in the wild.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reported; all trade specimens are wild-caught. Wild reproduction is presumed to involve broadcast spawning of pelagic eggs on outer reef slopes. Mature males are distinguished from females by elongated lyre-shaped caudal-fin streamers.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page for Naso Tang (Naso lituratus). Provided adult size (18 in), min tank (180 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), dKH (8–12), specific gravity (1.020–1.025), care level (moderate), temperament (semi-aggressive), reef compatibility (yes), and diet (herbivore, marine seaweed staple).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, taxonomy (family Acanthuridae, order Acanthuriformes), max size (46 cm SL), Indo-Pacific distribution (East Indian Ocean from Christmas Island through Pacific to Hawaii, French Polynesia, Pitcairn; north to Japan, south to Great Barrier Reef and New Caledonia), depth range (0–90 m, typical 5–30 m), wild temperature (24.5–28.9°C), diet (leafy brown algae Sargassum, Dictyota; trophic level 2.3). Confirmed the Indian Ocean population is now recognized as a separate species, Naso elegans.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~18 in / 45 cm), min tank size (180 gal / 681 L), temperature (75–79°F / 24–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), diet (brown macroalgae), depth (15–300+ ft), semi-aggressive temperament toward other tangs, and juvenile vs adult coloration.

additional.name: TFH Magazine
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (18 in), tank size guidance (75 gal minimum, 200+ gal preferred), diet (herbivore staple of marine algae/seaweed plus opportunistic frozen meaty foods), reef safety (will not bother sessile invertebrates), and sexual dimorphism (males develop lyre-shaped caudal extensions, juveniles gray darkening to chestnut-brown adults). Used as fallback for husbandry context.

additional.name: Fishmetry
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (45 cm), minimum tank (185 gal / 700 L), temperature (24–27°C), pH (8.1–8.4), specific gravity (1.020–1.026), reef safety (yes), and large active-swimmer husbandry framing. Lifespan estimate (20–40 years) is at the high end and was used as upper-bound context.

additional.name: Reef2Reef community thread (Naso Tang tank size requirements)
additional.notes: Specialist-keeper consensus that 180 gal is an absolute juvenile minimum, with adults requiring 240+ gal (~900 L) with an 8-ft footprint; serious keepers and public aquaria use 300–500 gal (~1100–1900 L). Drives the cautious 1000-L tank figure used here over the LiveAquaria 180-gal number, given the species' adult size and constant-swimming habit.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reported; all trade specimens are wild-caught. Mature males develop long lyre-shaped caudal-fin streamers, distinguishing them from females.
